California
Fair P01itical
Practices Cornrnission
October 23, l99O
Ariel Pierre cal.onne
city Attorney
250 Hamilton Avenue
Palo AIto, CA 94301
Re: Your Request for Advice
our File No. A-90-292
Dear Mr. Calonne:
This l-etter is in response to a request for advice subrnitted
to this office by Mr. Anthony c. Bennetti, forner acting city at-
torney for the city of Palo AIto, reqarding the duties-and
respois ibilities 
oi the members of the Sand Hill Corridor Joint
eol-icy committee (the ttcornmitteer') under the conf I ict-of-interest
provi-sions of the Political 
Reform Act (the "Agt'll.t- -9Yt response
iakes into consideration your letters of August 24, 1990, and
August 3l-, 199o, endorsj.ng Mr. Bennetti's request for advice,
a15ng with the additional information you have submitted for our
cons iderat ion .
OUESTION
1. The Sand Hitl Corridor Joint Policy Cornmittee is composed
of representatives of the cities of Pafo Alto and Menlo Park along
with iepresentatives of Stanford University, a private landowner.
fne cornitittee will act as an advisory body to the city councils of
the two cities involved in the project. 
Can each of two
tov"rnrnental agencies allow les! than a quorum of.their members to
io... 
conmitt6e with a private landowner to consider recommenda-
ii""" 
t" the governing bodies of those Local agencies without be-
i"g r"bl."t 
t6 tne diiclosure and disqua l if icatj'on provisions of
the Act?
2.HaStheSandHillCorridorJointPolicyConnitteereached
tire point wnere its decj'sions are routinely accepted by the. city
"orrrriil without substantial arnendment or modification such that it
snouraadoptaconflict-of-interestcodeoritsnon-e].ected
1 Go.r"rrr."nt Code Sections 81OOO-91015. All- statutory references
aretotheGovernmentcodeunlessotherwiseindicated'Conmission
iegutations appear at 2 California code of Regulations. Section
ia6oo; .i scs.- AIl references to regulations are to Title 2'
Oivision o of the californj-a code of Regulations'
428 J Street, Suite 8OO . P.O. Box 8O7 o sacrarnento cA 958O4-O8O7 . (916)322'5660


Fi■e No.
Page 2
members
code?
A-90-292
be added to the City of Palo Alto conflict-of-interest
CONCLUS IONS
1. The Act does not govern the membership of advisory com-
nittees. 
Hohrever, srhen an advisory cornnittee makes substantive
recommendatj-ons to a governmental agency and these reconmendations
are regularly approved without significant rnodification, the
rnemberl of the conmittee are public offi.cials subject to the
disclosure and disqual ification provisions of the Act. Moreover,
Palo Alto and lilenlo Park counci l-rnernbers s/ho are also nernbers of
the committee continue to be subject to the conf t ict-of- interest
provisions of the Act and nay not influence governmental decisions
in which they have financial- interests.
2. The Sand Hilt corridor Joint Policy Committee has made
certain recolnrnendations which the Palo Alto city council has
adopted. Ho\"rever, j,n each instance, these recommendations have
been adopted or amended after public hearinqs and extensive
deliberalions by the city councj.l. The city council' has analyzed
and debated the issues presented by the commj.ttee, requested
advice fron staff, and directed the cornmj-ttee to proceed according
to guidelines provided by the city council. Thus, the members of
the committee are not public officials subject to the disclosure
and disqual ification provisions of the Act at this time.
FACTS
Sand Hill Road runs roughly east-west fron Interstate Highway
280 through the city of Menlo Park (MenLo Park) in san tlateo
county and on through the City of Palo AIto (Palo Alto) in Santa
clara county to its intersection with Arboretum Road and thence on
by a circuitous route to EL camino Real- by way of the parking Iot
of Stanford Shopping center which is privately or./ned by the
Trustees of Leland Stanford, Jr. University (Stanford). Stanford
owns a najority of the Land inmediately adjacent to the north and
south of the road along the entire length j,n question, rnuch of it
undeveloped. over the years there have been various discussions
and proposal"s under a variety of names and sponsorships for
extending Sand Hill Road in some nanner to connect more directly
$rith EI canino Rea1. The proposals have generally been
controversial and no inprovement project has been successfully
irnplenented .
fn 1987, Palo AIto, t1enlo Park, and stanford formulated a
proposal for the Sand Hill corridor Joint Policy comnittee. Two
Palo AIto councilrnembers, tlro Menlo Park councilmembers, and two
offici,als frorn Stanford met to discuss a rrcharterrr and a
procedural framework for the committee. The charter and the
procedural franework were referred to the city councils of Palo
elto ana Menlo Park for approval. The councils of both cities


File No. A-9O-292
Page 3
approved the proposals and the committee began meeting froro time
to tine.
You indicate in your letter of August 31, 1990, that the con-
nittee partially fulfil.ls 
the early public consultation provisions
of the California Environrnental Quality Act, -Public Resources code
Section 2LOoo, et seq. and the state CEQA Guidelines, California
Code of Regrulations, Title 14, sections 15oo0, gE seo. By provid-
ing for public input at the early stages of a project, the conmit-
tea fulfills 
important state policies catling for public
participation in the environmental review Process.
Additionally, attached to your office's request for advice is
a menorandum from your office to the Palo Alto city Manager dated
January 30, 1990. The document discusses the history and status
of the committee as of January, 1990, and incl-udes a copy of the
charter. 
The PaIo Alto city Attorney has concluded that the Sand
Hill Corridor Joj.nt Policy committee does not possess decision-
rnaking authority within the meaning of Regulation 18700 (copy
enclosed ) .
The charter defines the role of the cornmittee as follows:
During the first Part of the project the com-
rnittee will represent the jurisdictions in the
discussions with the consultant. The committee
wilL review the work of the consultant and recom-
mend to their respective jurisdictions whether or
not to proceed as called for in the 'rFrameworkrr.
In addition, the committee !'rilL have the fo1-
lowing responsibilities :
1. The Committee will serve as a liaison to
the governing body of the respective jurisdictions.
2. The comnittee wil-L identify policy level
decisions that should be referred to the governing
bodies for acceptance.
3. The comrnittee wiLl provide a forun for the
presentation of views from the public, as the
project moves into the later stages.
4. The comnittee will nonitor the progress of
the project and be responsible for coordinating the
tining of various stages .
5. The Conrnittee will review and recomrnend
financing plans for the project,2
2 charter, Sand Hill corridor Joint policy Comrnittee, June 3,
1987.


File No. L-9o-292
Page 4
In subsequent correspondence you have subnj-tted to our atten-
tion minutes of rneetings of the Pal.o Al-to city council and staff
reports pertinent to the actj,vities of the committee. rnformation
contained in these materials indicates that since the January
memorandum of the city attorney, the comrnittee has consj'dered the
proposals of environmental impact report consultants, whj.ch were
screened by city of Palo Alto staff, and is ready to make a recotn-
nendation iegarding the environmental irnpact report to the city
councils of Palo AIto and Menlo Park.
ANALYSI S
Section 87100 prohibits a public officiaL from rnaking,
participating in naking, or in any !/ay atternpting to influence a
governmental decision in which the official 
knows or has reason to
know he or she has a financial interest. 
In furtherance of the
prohibitions of Section 87100, each state or local government
lgency must adopt a conf l ict-of- interest code' (Section.87300. )
conflict-of-interest 
codes are required to identify t'designated
employeesrr and assign to those designated positions appropriate
calegories of f inancj,al disclosure. 
Each conflict-of-interest
code is required to specificalLy enumerate the positions lrithin
the agency which involve making or participating in naking
governnenlal decisions whi.ch nay foreseeably have a naterial
iinancial effect on any economj-c interest. 
(Section a73o2. ) 
The
city of Palo AIto is a focal government agency and it has adopted
a conf l ict-of - interest code.
we no!, proceed to determine whether members of the cornrnittee
are designatLd employees who should be included in the city's
conflictlof- interest code. The tern ttdesignated employeerr is
defined in Section 82019 to specifically exclude unsalar.ied
members of boards or comrnissions which serve a solelv advisorv
function. Regulation L87oo(a) (1) provides guidelines in determin-
ing whether a board or commission is "sole1y advisorytr or has
decision-rnaking authority. 
It provides:
ttPublic official 
at any level of state or Io-
ca1 governnentrt neans every natural person who is a
member, officer, employee or consultant of a state
or 1ocal government agrency.
trlttemberrr sha1l include, but not be linited to,
salaried or unsalaried members of boards or commis-
sions with decision-rnaking authority. 
A board or
cornmission possesses decision-rnaking authority
whenever :
(A)
sion;
It may make a final governrnentaL deci-
(B) It may cornpel a governmental decision; or
it nay prevent a governrnental decision either by


Fi■e No. A-90-292
Page 5
reason of an exclusive power to initiate the deci-
sion or by reason of a veto which may not be
overridenl or
(c) It makes substantive reconmendations
which are, and over an extended period of tine have
been, regularly approved without significant amend-
ment or nodification by another public official 
or
governmental agencY.
Regulation 18700 (a) (1).
Applying this regulation to your facts, we conclude that the
cornmitlee does not possesses decision-makj.ng authority, because it
does not make substantive recornmendations whj,ch are regularly ap-
proved without significant rnodification by the city council of the
city of Pato AIto. In general-, the role of the committee has been
to iecommend a procedural framework work plan for inplementation
of the proj ect.
The connittee's charter states, inter alia, that the cornrnit-
tee wifl review and recommend financing plans for the project and
identify policy leve1 decisions to be referred to the city
council! ior acceptance. The purpose of the committee is to adopt
a framework which wi]l constitute the $rork plan for the project.
Pursuant to the provisions of its charter, the comrnittee has, at
the dj.rection of the city counciL, contracted for the services of
a consultant selected and recommended by city staff. 
Under the
direction of the committee, this consultant has developed
procedural, guidelines for the project. 
At all times, the recom-
rnendations of the committee have been subject to extensive
deliberation by the City Council of the City of Palo AIto. 
The
public has been afforded ampl-e opportunity to comment on each
recommendation. The cornmittee does not rnake, conpel, or prevent
any final decisions. (See fn re Rotman (1987) 10 FPPC ops. 1,
copy enclosed. ) It is merely analyzing alternatives for comple-
tion of the project and subnitting its findings to the Palo AIto
City Council for consideration.
So far, all the recommendations of the comrnittee have been
approved by the city council of the city of Palo Alto upon subnit-
taI. 
However, as the minutes of the Palo Alto city council
indicate, pri.or to adoption of a recomnendation as submitted by
the conmittee, there has been extensive debate, input fron the
public, and, with one exception, modifications and amendments to
the committee,s recommendations prior to adoption. For exanple,
on November L2, 1987 , the connittee reviewed and adopted a
procedural framework rrork plan developed by staff. 
The purpose of
the document is to identify and describe a process to be followed
for the completion of the Sand Hill corridor project. 
This
procedural frarnework was approved, in arnended forn, by the city
council of the City of Palo Alto on January 4, LgBB, and by the
city council of the City of Menlo Park on February 2, 1988.


File No. A-9o-292
Page 5
On May 16, 1988, the committee reviewed the staff reconmenda-
tion of a consultant to develop the procedural. framework for the
Sand Hill Project. 
The contract was submitted to the Palo Alto
city council for approval. It appears that the Palo Alto city
Council approved the reconmendation of the committee without
urodification.
on December 1-2, L988, the Palo Alto City council held study
sessions on conceptual alternatives for possible Sand Hill, Road
extensions. The corTunittee had identif j.ed seven possible alterna-
tives for irnplementing the project and other alternatives were
under consideration. No decisj,ons had yet been made as to which
aLternatives to pursue or irnplement. The Palo Al,to city Council
endorsed three conceptual alternatives for future EIR evaluation,
directed the committee and the consultant to proceed $rith Part B
of the Procedural Framework work Program, and approved outlined
procedures for a possible Sand HiIl Road Extension EIR and
prelirninary design phase. As recomnended by staff, the Palo Alto
city council- also approved a budget arnendment increasing funding
for the procedural franework and approved modification to the
consulting services agreement expanding the scope of work in part
B of the Procedural Franework.
on March 13, 1989, the Palo Al-to city council conducted a
public hearing and dj.scussed the recommendations subrnitted by the
cornrnittee. The consulting agreement for the procedural framework
was approved as amended by the city council. The consulting
agreement was arnended once again on April 10, 1989, and on
December 4, 1989.
We conclude, based upon the information submitted to us, that
the cornmittee is at present sotely advisory in nature and its
menbers are not subject to the conflict-of-interest 
provisions of
the Act. The committee has submitted various recommendations to
the Palo Atto City Council, but these recommendations have been
scrutj,nized and arnended prior to approval. while it j-s a close
case, we believe, on balance, that the committee has not
established a record sufficient to show that the cornmittee is a
decision-rnaking body pursuant to ReguLation 18700(a) (1) (c).
Because the committee does not have decision-making authority, its
nembers are not subject to the conf I ict-of-interest 
provisions of
the Act.
we note, however, that this advice is subject to change as
the project develops. According to the information provided, some
reconmendations of the comrnittee have been approved without
significant amendment or nodification. 
If a pattern of such ap-
proval occurs, it is likely that the cornmittee would become a
decision-making body for purposes of the Act. For exanple, if the
purpose of the committee is to provide input in the initial
stages, the conmittee would not be considered a decis ion-uraking
body. However, if the committee continues to exist as the project
develops and its input constitutes an on-going process, then the
comrnittee r./ou1d be cons j,dered a decision-making body for purposes


File No. A-9O-292
Page 7
of the Act. In this event, the committee would be required to
adoDt a conflict-of-interest 
code and its rtembers would be subject
to Lne conf l ict-of- interest provisions of the Act.
Moreover, as stated above, Section 87100 prohibits any public
official 
from making, participating in, or attempting to use his
or her official 
position to influence any governmental decision in
which he or she knows, or has reason to know, he or she has a
financial interest. 
At the present tine, being a nember of the
Sand Hill Corridor Joint Policy conmittee does not in and of
itself nake one a ttpubLic officialrt for purposes of the Act.
Holrever, it nust be remembered that members of the Palo Alto city
Council are public official,s and continue to be subject to the
conflict-of-interest 
provisions of the Act. Thus, menbers of the
Palo AIto city council who are also menbers of the committee must
refrain from iaking, participatJ.ng in, or attenPting to use their
official 
position to inrluence any governmental decision in which
they have a financial interest.
We trust this letter adeguatel,y responds to your inquiry.
Should you have any further questions regarding this rnatter, do
not hesitate to call ne at (916) 322-59oL.
S incerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting General counsel

procedures for a possible Sand HiIl Road Extension EIR and
prelirninary design phase. As recomnended by staff, the Palo Alto
city council- also approved a budget arnendment increasing funding
for the procedural franework and approved modification to the
consulting services agreement expanding the scope of work in part
B of the Procedural Franework.
on March 13, 1989, the Palo Al-to city council conducted a
public hearing and dj.scussed the recommendations subrnitted by the
cornrnittee. The consulting agreement for the procedural framework
was approved as amended by the city council. The consulting
agreement was arnended once again on April 10, 1989, and on
December 4, 1989.
We conclude, based upon the information submitted to us, that
the cornmittee is at present sotely advisory in nature and its
menbers are not subject to the conflict-of-interest 
provisions of
the Act. The committee has submitted various recommendations to
the Palo Atto City Council, but these recommendations have been
scrutj,nized and arnended prior to approval. while it j-s a close
case, we believe, on balance, that the committee has not
established a record sufficient to show that the cornmittee is a
decision-rnaking body pursuant to ReguLation 18700(a) (1) (c).
Because the committee does not have decision-making authority, its
nembers are not subject to the conf I ict-of-interest 
provisions of
the Act.
we note, however, that this advice is subject to change as
the project develops. According to the information provided, some
reconmendations of the comrnittee have been approved without
significant amendment or nodification. 
If a pattern of such ap-
proval occurs, it is likely that the cornmittee would become a
decision-making body for purposes of the Act. For exanple, if the
purpose of the committee is to provide input in the initial
stages, the conmittee would not be considered a decis ion-uraking
body. However, if the committee continues to exist as the project
develops and its input constitutes an on-going process, then the
comrnittee r./ou1d be cons j,dered a decision-making body for purposes


File No. A-9O-292
of the Act. In this event, the committee would be required to
adoDt a conflict-of-interest 
code and its rtembers would be subject
to Lne conf l ict-of- interest provisions of the Act.
Moreover, as stated above, Section 87100 prohibits any public
official 
from making, participating in, or attempting to use his
or her official 
position to influence any governmental decision in
which he or she knows, or has reason to know, he or she has a
financial interest. 
At the present tine, being a nember of the
Sand Hill Corridor Joint Policy conmittee does not in and of
itself nake one a ttpubLic officialrt for purposes of the Act.
Holrever, it nust be remembered that members of the Palo Alto city
Council are public official,s and continue to be subject to the
conflict-of-interest 
provisions of the Act. Thus, menbers of the
Palo AIto city council who are also menbers of the committee must
refrain from iaking, participatJ.ng in, or attenPting to use their
official 
position to inrluence any governmental decision in which
they have a financial interest.
We trust this letter adeguatel,y responds to your inquiry.
